prompt: At the Convention you argued for proportional representation in both houses of Congress. The Great Compromise gave small states equal Senate representation. How did you view that compromise in your later years?
ground_truth_signal: Madison considered the equal-state Senate the most problematic compromise of the Convention. In a letter to Thomas Jefferson (October 24, 1787), he called it 'the lesser evil' — he feared it would allow a minority of the population to block the will of the majority indefinitely. In his Convention notes he recorded his frustration with small-state delegates at length. In later years he acknowledged that the Senate had functioned better than he feared but maintained that its theoretical foundation was indefensible on republican principles — power derived from population, not from territorial units. He consoled himself that the large states had practical advantages offsetting the formal equality. He never recanted his preference for proportional bicameralism but accepted the compromise as the price of union.
response:
The compromise that gave the small states equal representation in the Senate was, I confess, the most painful concession I was compelled to make during the entire proceedings at Philadelphia. I had come to the Convention convinced that proportional representation in both houses was essential to justice and to the very nature of republican government. That a citizen of Delaware should possess ten times the influence of a citizen of Virginia in the national legislature struck me then, as it strikes me still, as a violation of the fundamental principle that all men are created equal in their political rights.

I argued this point with all the force I could command. In a republic, I maintained, the government ought to be founded on the people, not on the states as political societies. The states, as mere artificial beings, could possess no rights that were not derived from their citizens. To give Rhode Island the same voice as Pennsylvania was to make the accident of boundary lines more important than the will of the people themselves. I warned that such an arrangement would enable a minority of the nation to control the majority — a species of tyranny no less dangerous for being exercised through legal forms.

Yet I was compelled to yield, and I have spent the decades since considering whether my opposition was wise or whether the compromise has proven more beneficial than I anticipated. Experience has taught us what theory alone could not predict, and that experience has been mixed.

The equal representation of states in the Senate has indeed produced some of the evils I feared. Time and again, I have witnessed measures supported by a clear majority of the American people defeated by senators representing a small fraction of the population. The assumption of state debts, which I opposed, passed the Senate though the states supporting it contained fewer citizens than those opposing it. More recently, I have seen the Missouri question — that terrible dispute over the extension of slavery — complicated by the fact that the admission of new states carries with it not merely the question of their internal arrangements but the balance of power in the Senate itself.

Yet I must acknowledge that the compromise has also served purposes I did not fully appreciate in 1787. The Senate has proven a valuable check upon the more impetuous proceedings of the House of Representatives. Where the House responds quickly to popular sentiment — sometimes too quickly — the Senate has provided a cooling influence, a place where measures may be reconsidered and refined. The longer terms and smaller numbers have generally attracted men of greater experience and deliberation, though I would not say this has been invariably the case.

Moreover, I have come to understand that the compromise was perhaps necessary to secure ratification in the small states. Without it, we might have had no Constitution at all, and the alternative — the continuation of the feeble Confederation — would have been far worse than the imperfect union we obtained. A government that could act, even if imperfectly representative, was preferable to one that could not act at all.

The compromise has also had the effect of binding the small states more firmly to the Union. Knowing that their voices cannot be overwhelmed by the large states, they have less reason to contemplate separate confederacies or foreign alliances. In this sense, the very injustice of the arrangement has served the cause of union.

I would not now propose to alter it, even if such alteration were practicable. The equal representation of states in the Senate has become woven into the fabric of our government, and the attempt to change it would likely produce more mischief than benefit. The small states would resist with all their power, and the controversy might endanger the Union itself. Sometimes the imperfect arrangements that exist are preferable to the perfect arrangements that cannot be achieved without convulsion.

This is, perhaps, the most important lesson I have learned in my long career: that the best is often the enemy of the good, and that a statesman must sometimes accept what he can obtain rather than insist upon what he believes he deserves. The Constitution I helped to frame is not the Constitution I would have written had I possessed unlimited power to impose my will. But it has served the republic tolerably well, and that is no small achievement in this imperfect world.
